query: management of hepatitis C in patients with cirrhosis

retrieved_docs.title: Viral Hepatitis -- Treatment / Management -- Hepatitis C
retrieved_docs.content: Treatment of HCV infection may also be divided into acute HCV infection and chronic HCV infection.

retrieved_docs.title: Viral Hepatitis -- Treatment / Management -- Chronic hepatitis C infection
retrieved_docs.content: Those patients who are treatment-experienced, have decompensated cirrhosis, or are otherwise part of a unique population (HCV with HIV coinfection, solid organ transplant recipient, organs from HCV-viremic donors) require more complex evaluation and management and should be referred for expert care. Recommendations for management are outlined in the updated guidance by the joint association of The Infectious Diseases Society of America and the American Association for the Study of Liver Diseases. [45] Dynamic changes in recommendation can be viewed online at https://www.hcvguidelines.org/.

retrieved_docs.title: Viral Hepatitis -- Treatment / Management -- Chronic hepatitis C infection
retrieved_docs.content: Since the introduction of the second generation of direct-acting antiviral (DAA) agents in 2013 and 2014, interferon-based therapy has become obsolete. It is now contraindicated in the management of HCV infection. Ribavirin use can be considered in particular circumstances, such as decompensated cirrhosis or those with viral resistance to prior therapy. With the availability of an all-oral regimen that is well tolerated and minimal pretreatment testing required, an increased number of patients are eligible for anti-HCV treatment.

retrieved_docs.title: Hepatitis C -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: The standard regimens are anywhere from 12 weeks to 24 weeks with or without ribavirin based on the genotype, treatment experience, and presence or absence of cirrhosis. With the current DAAs, the Genotype 3 infection, which is the least responsive, is associated with rapid accelerated fibrosis progression and a higher incidence of hepatocellular carcinoma. Genotype 1, the most common genotype in the United States, has 4 treatments approved, 2 of which require only a single pill per day. For example, the combination of sofosbuvir and ledipasvir in a single pill and the combination of grazoprevir and elbasvir as a single pill. Many more drugs in phase III and IV clinical trials appear to have a pan-genotypic potential such that it eliminate the need to check the HCV genotype. Defining each regimen for the various genotypes is beyond the scope of this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Viral Hepatitis -- Treatment / Management -- Chronic hepatitis C infection
retrieved_docs.content: The primary goal of treatment for chronic HCV infection is the eradication of HCV. A cure of the viral infection, also known as a sustained virologic response (SVR), is defined as the lack of viral detection in the blood 12 weeks after receiving anti-HCV therapy. Patients who achieve this endpoint have a 99% or greater chance of maintaining the cure with a later appearance of viremia attributed to reinfection. [46] Cure of the hepatitis C infection reverses liver inflammation, fibrosis, and associated changes related to portal hypertension. [47] [48] There is also ample evidence that the elimination of HCV results in a decreased risk of developing hepatocellular carcinoma and an overall reduction in all-cause mortality. [48] [49] Patients also generally report improvement in symptoms of fatigue and general well-being. Treatment of HCV infection is also an indirect path to treating extrahepatic manifestations of the virus, such as glomerulonephropathies, cryoglobulinemic vasculitis, and non-Hodgkin lymphoma. The treatment regimen selection is determined by the patient's treatment history (whether naive or experienced), the degree of fibrosis or the presence of cirrhosis, and whether there are any complications of cirrhosis. Additional treatment selection considerations are given to unique patient populations such as those with HIV/HCV coinfection, decompensated cirrhosis, those after liver or kidney transplant, children, and during pregnancy.

retrieved_docs.title: Hepatitis C -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Hepatitis C virus (HCV) is a major public health problem affecting 58 million people worldwide. The percentage of people who are seropositive for anti-HCV antibodies worldwide is estimated to have increased from 2.3% to 2.8% between 1990 and 2005. Most patients (80% to 85%) who become acutely infected cannot clear the virus and progress to chronic infection. The effects of chronic infection include cirrhosis, portal hypertension, hepatic decompensation with encephalopathy, and hepatocellular carcinoma. The treatment landscape has evolved substantially since introducing highly active direct-acting antivirals (DAAs) in 2011. This activity reviews the pathophysiology, evaluation, and management of hepatitis C and highlights the interprofessional team's role in managing affected patients.

retrieved_docs.title: Hepatitis C -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Identify the epidemiology of hepatitis C. Assess the typical presentation of a patient with hepatitis C. Differentiate the treatment and management options available for hepatitis C. Review interprofessional team strategies for improving care coordination and communication to advance the treatment of hepatitis C and improve patient outcomes. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Hepatic Cirrhosis -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Damage to the liver is permanent. Nevertheless, further injury to the liver should be avoided to halt the progression of the disease. General management to prevent chronic liver disease includes avoidance of alcohol, vaccination for HBV and HCV, good nutrition with a balanced diet, weight reduction, and early treatment of precipitating factors like dehydration, hypotension, and infections (see Image. Schistosomiasis Infection). This is achieved by routine monitoring of volume status, kidney function, varices development, and progression to HCC. Specific therapy usually targets the etiology, including antiviral medications in viral hepatitis, steroids and immunosuppressant agents in autoimmune hepatitis, ursodeoxycholic acid and obeticholic acid in primary biliary cholangitis, copper chelation in Wilson disease, and iron chelation and phlebotomy in hemochromatosis. Weight loss of at least 7% is beneficial in NASH, and alcohol abstinence is crucial in alcoholic cirrhosis. [28]

retrieved_docs.title: Viral Hepatitis -- Treatment / Management -- Acute hepatitis C infection
retrieved_docs.content: Acute HCV infection is infrequently detected as most cases are asymptomatic. However, when detected, immediate treatment is recommended. [45] The treatment is similar to that outlined below for chronic HCV infection.

retrieved_docs.title: Hepatitis C -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: First diagnosed in 1989, the hepatitis C virus (HCV) is a significant public health problem affecting 58 million people worldwide. The percentage of people who are seropositive for anti-HCV antibodies worldwide is estimated to have increased from 2.3% to 2.8% between 1990 and 2005. Most patients (80% to 85%) who become acutely infected cannot clear the virus and progress to chronic infection. The effects of chronic infection include cirrhosis, portal hypertension, hepatic decompensation with encephalopathy, and hepatocellular carcinoma. HCV is the most common blood-borne pathogen and a leading cause of morbidity and mortality. The treatment landscape has evolved substantially since introducing highly active direct-acting antivirals (DAAs) in 2011. The goals of treatment aim at viral eradication, delaying fibrosis progression, alleviating symptoms, preventing complications, minimizing all-cause mortality, and ultimately maximizing the quality of life. [1] [2] [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Viral Hepatidities (Archived) -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Direct-acting antivirals (DAA’s) are the treatment of choice for HCV infection. However, different genotypes respond better to certain DAA’s than others. Furthermore, the decision to treat a patient at the presentation of acute infection versus monitoring to see if the disease becomes chronic and then treating is another issue. This will be explained in the HCV specific review topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Viral Hepatitis -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: Liver-directed treatment of complications is, in large part, related to the complications of cirrhosis and is best managed by experts in cirrhosis care. Liver transplantation is curative for the complications of cirrhosis in eligible patients.

retrieved_docs.title: Viral Hepatitis -- Treatment / Management -- Chronic hepatitis C infection
retrieved_docs.content: For most treatment-naive patients without cirrhosis, the preferred regimen includes the pan-genotypic option of glecaprevir/pibrentasvir administered as 3 combination pills 3 times daily for 8 weeks or sofosbuvir/velpatasvir administered as 1 pill daily for 12 weeks. The choice of regimen is mainly influenced by the preference for the duration of therapy versus the number of pills and payer coverage. Both regimens have sustained virologic response (SVR) rates exceeding 95% and are well tolerated. Both combinations are safe to use in patients with renal insufficiency; glecaprevir/pibrentasvir should be avoided in decompensated cirrhosis. There are some potential severe drug interactions with either regimen, so it is necessary to run a drug interaction check before prescribing so that any necessary adjustments can be made. Additional regimens include sofosbuvir/daclatasvir (for genotypes 1, 2, 3, and 4), ledipasvir/sofosbuvir (for genotypes 1, 4, 5, and 6), and elbasvir/grazoprevir (for genotypes 1 and 4). These second-line agents require additional genotype and resistance testing and are indicated for patients with specific favorable clinical profiles. Sofosbuvir/daclatasvir is not available in the United States. These factors render them suboptimal options for consideration.

retrieved_docs.title: Hepatic Cirrhosis -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: An interprofessional team that includes a hepatologist, gastroenterologist, liver surgeon, pathologist, infectious disease specialist, primary care provider, and internist is best for treating and preventing liver cirrhosis. All healthcare workers should follow patients with liver dysfunction from any cause because it can quickly become irreversible. Liver cirrhosis is associated with many systemic complications that can cause death. A liver transplant is not always an option because of the shortage of donors.

retrieved_docs.title: Portal Hypertension -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: Early identification and management, including NSBB, endoscopic interventions, and treatment of the underlying cause (eg, antivirals for hepatitis C or anticoagulation for thrombosis), can improve outcomes. Abstinence from alcohol may reverse some complications, while persistent or severe disease may require TIPS or liver transplantation, the only definitive cure for cirrhosis-related portal hypertension. Delayed diagnosis or inadequate management leads to increased hospitalization, higher mortality, and a greater need for transplant evaluation.

retrieved_docs.title: Chronic Liver Disease -- Treatment / Management -- General Management
retrieved_docs.content: Patients with chronic liver disease mostly present with one of the complications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 3 -- Community 2274
retrieved_docs.content: Alcoholic cirrhosis (تَشَمَّعٌ كُحولِيّ) is a specific subtype of liver cirrhosis (تَشَمُّع كَبِدي). Liver cirrhosis, in turn, is clinically associated with esophageal variceal bleeding (نزيف دَوالِي المريء). Recognizing that patients with alcoholic cirrhosis develop the broader condition of liver cirrhosis helps clinicians anticipate the high risk of variceal hemorrhage, prompting appropriate screening, monitoring, and preventive interventions to improve patient outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 1 -- Community 210
retrieved_docs.content: modalities, procedural interventions, and disease processes are anatomically and clinically related, providing a comprehensive framework for the evaluation and management of abdominal, hepatic, and biliary disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 2 -- Community 655
retrieved_docs.content: Antivirals for treatment of HCV infections include ribavirin and telaprevir, both of which are employed as therapeutic agents against hepatitis C virus. Remdesivir has been identified as a medication that may treat عدوى فيروسية (viral infection), while ribavirin is also noted for its potential to prevent عدوى فيروسية. Together, these antiviral drugs illustrate the spectrum of strategies used to both treat and prevent viral diseases, underscoring their importance in clinical management of infections such as hepatitis C and other viral pathogens.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 3 -- Community 2620
retrieved_docs.content: Alcoholic hepatitis (تَشَمَّعٌ كُحولِيّ) is a liver disorder caused by excessive alcohol consumption that can progress to hepatic cirrhosis (تَشَمُّع كَبِدي). This relationship highlights the potential evolution from an inflammatory, potentially reversible condition to an irreversible stage of extensive liver scarring, emphasizing the importance of early detection and treatment of alcoholic hepatitis to prevent the development of cirrhosis.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 285
retrieved_docs.content: Ribavirin is classified as a member of the group of antivirals used for the treatment of hepatitis C virus (HCV) infections, indicating its role within this therapeutic class. In addition to its use against HCV, ribavirin may also help prevent viral infections (عدوى فيروسية). Remdesivir, another antiviral agent, may be employed to treat viral infections (عدوى فيروسية). Together, ribavirin and remdesivir exemplify antiviral medications that are utilized both for treating specific viral diseases such as HCV and for broader management of viral infections, underscoring their importance in antiviral therapy and patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 2 -- Community 262
retrieved_docs.content: The therapeutic category “Antivirals for treatment of HCV infections” comprises agents such as ribavirin and telaprevir, both of which are employed as antiviral medications specifically to manage hepatitis C virus (HCV) infection.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 1 -- Community 228
retrieved_docs.content: SMQ. Hepatic cirrhosis and portal hypertension predispose patients to دوالي المَريء, which can progress to نزيف دَوالِي المريء (esophageal variceal bleeding). This bleeding is a major cause of نَزِف الجزء العلوي من الجهاز الهضمي (upper gastrointestinal hemorrhage) and is linked to نزيف الدوالي الوريدية (variceal bleeding). Overall, the framework links esophageal variceal pathology, its underlying vascular morphology, associated bleeding manifestations, alcohol‑related liver disease, portal hypertension, and the endoscopic therapeutic procedures used for diagnosis, classification, and patient‑centered management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 1 -- Community 213
retrieved_docs.content: cirrhosis (تشَمُّع كَبِدي) and is associated with esophageal variceal bleeding. The knowledge base therefore integrates congenital correction, diagnostic imaging, endoscopic therapeutic techniques, variceal pathophysiology, and related hepatic risk factors, emphasizing the interconnected role of vascular morphology, procedural categories, and clinical associations in the management of esophageal and related gastrointestinal diseases.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 2341
retrieved_docs.content: Failure (biologic function) is associated with الفَشَلٌ الكَبِدِيُّ الكُلْوِيّ, indicating that a general loss of biological function can be linked to hepatorenal failure—a severe condition in which both liver and kidney functions are compromised. Recognizing this association is important for clinicians, as it highlights the need to assess and manage systemic organ failure comprehensively in affected patients.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 2 -- Community 638
retrieved_docs.content: Antivirals for treatment of HCV infections are classified as direct‑acting antivirals, and telaprevir is a member of this antiviral group used specifically to treat hepatitis C virus infection.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 4 -- Community 2842
retrieved_docs.content: Esophageal disorders are classified under the category “اضطرابات مَريئِيّة أخرى” (other esophageal disorders). Within this classification, the SMQ titled “الفَشَل الكَبِديّ، والتَلَيُّف وتَشَمُّعُ الكَبِد والحالات الأخرى المرتبطة بتلف الكبد” (liver failure, hepatic fibrosis, cirrhosis, and other conditions associated with liver damage) is listed as a member of the esophageal disorders group. Although liver pathology is not a primary manifestation of esophageal disease, this coding framework groups these hepatic conditions as members of the broader esophageal disorder category, likely to capture related comorbidities or overlapping clinical presentations. Recognizing this relationship helps clinicians and medical coders document and manage patients who present with both esophageal and liver abnormalities in an integrated manner.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 1993
retrieved_docs.content: Abnormal chest X‑ray findings (الأشعة السينية على الصَدْر غير طبيعية) are clinically associated with alcoholic cirrhosis (تَشَمَّعٌ كُحولِيّ). In patients with alcoholic cirrhosis, chest radiographs frequently show atypical patterns that may reflect complications such as pulmonary edema, infections, or pleural effusions, underscoring the importance of evaluating chest imaging when managing this liver disease.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 1 -- Community 246
retrieved_docs.content: دوالي المريء (variceal ligation) and مفاغرة مريئية (esophageal sclerotherapy). These interventions aim to control hemorrhage from varices secondary to portal hypertension related to liver disease and alcohol‑induced hepatic injury. Understanding the interconnected network of dilated‑vein morphology (وريد متدلي), anatomical sites (مفاغرة مريئية), procedural approaches, and etiological factors (alcohol use, liver disease) is essential for accurate diagnosis, risk stratification, and comprehensive patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 2 -- Community 369
retrieved_docs.content: Ribavirin is an antiviral medication that belongs to the class of antivirals used for the treatment of hepatitis C virus (HCV) infections, making it a member of the therapeutic group “Antivirals for treatment of HCV infections.”

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 1 -- Community 72
retrieved_docs.content: includes upper gastrointestinal X‑ray, while other non‑OR upper GI therapeutic procedures encompass a range of endoscopic and radiologic interventions. Overall, the knowledge base integrates congenital esophageal repair, endoscopic dilatation, variceal management, lower‑extremity venous disease, and their common etiologic factors (alcohol, liver disease) to guide comprehensive patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 1 -- Community 215
retrieved_docs.content: (alcoholic cirrhosis), which belong to the SMQ encompassing الفَشَل الكَبِديّ، والتَلَيُّف وتَشَمُّعُ الكَبِد (liver failure, fibrosis, cirrhosis, and other liver‑damage conditions). Therapeutic interventions specific to the esophagus—ربط دوالي المريء (esophageal variceal ligation) and مفاغرة مريئية (esophageal sclerotherapy)—are employed to control نزيف دَوالِي المريء (esophageal variceal bleeding) and related variceal hemorrhage. Recognizing these interconnections guides clinicians to identify patients at risk due to liver disease and alcohol use, understand the development of esophageal and gastric varices, and select appropriate endoscopic treatments for upper GI bleeding.
